summary:
The Coronation of Charlemagne (Christmas Day, December 25, 800 AD) — when Pope Leo III placed the imperial crown on the Frankish king's head in St. Peter's Basilica in Rome and the congregation acclaimed him 'Augustus' — was one of the most politically significant acts of the medieval period, creating the concept of a restored Western Roman Empire under papal authority and establishing the ideal of a universal Christian empire that would animate European politics for centuries. According to his biographer Einhard, Charlemagne was surprised by the coronation — but this claim of royal modesty is typically taken with skepticism by historians.

The coronation's implications were profound and contentious. It immediately angered the Byzantine Empire, which considered itself the only legitimate Roman Empire (Constantinople's empress Irene reportedly considered marrying Charlemagne to unite the two empires). It created the template for the Holy Roman Empire — the 'First Reich' — whose emperors from [[event:2932|Otto I]] onward would claim continuity with Charlemagne's coronation. Most consequentially, it established papal authority as the source of imperial legitimacy, a claim that would generate the Investiture Controversy and centuries of conflict between popes and emperors. The question of whether the pope could crown — and therefore also depose — emperors would dominate European political thought until the Reformation. The coronation's significance was recognized immediately by contemporaries as a claim that Christendom was a unified political entity with Rome (not Constantinople) at its spiritual center.
